threats.title: AVM-Delegated veNFT Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked in AutoVotingEscrow Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: Bribe.getReward() sends rewards to ownerOf(tokenId) which returns the AutoVotingEscrow (AVE) instance address for AVM-managed tokens. The check `_owner == avm` compares the AVE address against the AutoVotingEscrowManager address — always false. Rewards are transferred to the AVE contract which has zero ERC20 recovery functions. Additionally, setOriginalOwner in the manager is a no-op (empty body), and VotingEscrow._transferFrom's `_to == avm` check never triggers because the actual transfer destination is the AVE instance, not the manager. All bribe rewards for AVM-managed tokens are permanently lost.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a double-layered integration failure affecting every user who enables auto-voting. The AVE address != AVM manager address comparison guarantees the wrong code path. Even if patched, the no-op setOriginalOwner provides no fallback.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ownerOf returns AVE address; `_owner == avm` compares AVE vs Manager (always false); safeTransfer sends to AVE with no recovery

preconditions: User has enabled auto-voting for at least one veNFT
The AVM-managed veNFT has voted for pools with bribe rewards
gaugeManager calls getReward for the tokenId

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: AutoVotingEscrow holds NFTs but has no ERC20 withdrawal. Bribe.getReward sends tokens to ownerOf which is the AVE.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: setOriginalOwner is empty (line ~248 in AutoVotingEscrowManager); no admin action can fix already-locked funds
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — every bribe claim for AVM tokens triggers this loss

operational_questions: How many tokens are currently managed by AVM instances?
Have any bribe getReward calls already been made for AVM-managed tokens?
validation_requirements: Verify ownerOf(tokenId) returns AVE address, not AVM manager address
Confirm AutoVotingEscrow has no ERC20 transfer function
Verify setOriginalOwner function body is empty

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Any user calls enableAutoVoting(tokenId) — normal protocol usage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: AVM executor votes the token via performLockAction (normal operation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: gaugeManager calls getReward — rewards sent to AVE contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens are permanently locked in AVE with no recovery path
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — this occurs during normal protocol operation

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: All bribe rewards for AVM-delegated tokens are permanently lost
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: AVM feature becomes a value trap — users lose all bribe income
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible without contract upgrade; locked funds in immutable AVE contracts
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All bribe reward distribution for AVM-managed veNFTs

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None required — this is a systemic bug, not an exploit

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ERC20 balances accumulating in AutoVotingEscrow contracts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users enabling auto-voting stop receiving bribe rewards

threats.title: Bribe Reward Claims Permanently DoS'd by Unbounded earned() Loop with WEEK=1800
threats.hypothesis_description: The Bribe.earned() function iterates over every epoch since lastEarn with no iteration cap. With WEEK=1800 seconds (30 minutes), epochs accumulate at 48/day. After ~8-10 real-world days without claiming, earned() requires 384-480 iterations, each performing two binary searches over storage (getPriorBalanceIndex + getPriorSupplyIndex). At ~39,000 gas per iteration, this exceeds Avalanche's 15M block gas limit. Since lastEarn is only updated inside getReward() which calls earned(), the DoS is self-reinforcing and permanent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The combination of WEEK=1800 (extremely short epochs for production) and unbounded iteration creates a practical gas DoS after just days of inactivity. This affects any user who doesn't claim every few days.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++) loop with no upper bound; numEpochs = (epochStart(now) - lastEarnEpoch) / WEEK; WEEK=1800

preconditions: WEEK = 1800 seconds (confirmed in BlackTimeLibrary)
User hasn't claimed bribe rewards for ~8-10 days
Block gas limit is 15M (Avalanche C-chain)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: earned() loop count = (current_epoch - last_earn_epoch) / 1800; after 10 days = 480 iterations * ~39K gas/iter ≈ 18.7M gas > 15M limit
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin function can reset lastEarn or paginate claims
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Grows worse over time — once DoS'd, can never be un-DoS'd

operational_questions: What is the actual gas cost per iteration on Avalanche?
How many active bribe tokens exist (loop in getReward is per-token)?
Is there any mechanism to partially claim or advance lastEarn?
validation_requirements: Gas profile earned() with increasing epoch gaps
Confirm no alternative claim path exists
Test with realistic checkpoint counts for binary search depth

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User votes and earns bribe rewards (normal usage)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User doesn't claim for 8-10 days (passive — vacation, forgotten wallet, etc.)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: getReward call reverts due to out-of-gas in earned() loop
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Rewards are permanently locked — lastEarn never updates, loop only grows longer

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: All bribe rewards permanently locked for affected users
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Any user inactive for >8 days loses all accumulated bribe rewards forever
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible without contract upgrade; no admin recovery path
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Bribe reward distribution for all long-term holders

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None — affects normal users through inactivity

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Users unable to claim bribe rewards; revert traces showing out-of-gas
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Growing bribe token balances in Bribe contracts with no claims
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Sudden inability to claim after period of inactivity

threats.title: VotingEscrow Insolvency via Supply Desync from smNFT Token Burns
threats.hypothesis_description: When normal locks are merged into smNFTs (merge with smNFT target) or converted via updateToSMNFT, tokens are burned from the VotingEscrow contract via _black.burn(). However, the `supply` state variable is never decremented. Over time, VE's actual BLACK token balance becomes less than the sum of non-smNFT locked amounts. Normal lock holders attempting to withdraw will face reverts when IERC20(token).transfer fails due to insufficient balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The supply variable tracks deposits but not smNFT burns. Each merge of non-smNFT into smNFT burns value0 tokens from VE without adjusting supply. Cumulative burns create a growing gap between recorded supply and actual balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: In merge: `assert(_black.burn(value0))` burns tokens from VE balance but supply is not decremented; same pattern in updateToSMNFT line ~568

preconditions: Users create normal locks then merge into smNFTs
Cumulative burns exceed the difference between total deposits and normal lock withdrawals
Other users hold normal (non-permanent, non-smNFT) locks awaiting withdrawal

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: merge(normalLock, smNFTLock) → _black.burn(value0) reduces VE balance; supply unchanged; withdraw(otherNormalLock) → transfer(msg.sender, value) may fail
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin function to reconcile supply with actual balance
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Cumulative — grows with each normal→smNFT merge

operational_questions: How much cumulative value has been burned via smNFT merges?
What is the current VE token balance vs sum of non-smNFT locked amounts?
How many normal locks are pending withdrawal?
validation_requirements: Track cumulative _black.burn calls from VE
Compare IERC20(token).balanceOf(VE) with sum of withdrawable amounts
Verify supply variable exceeds actual balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker creates many normal locks with large amounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker merges all normal locks into smNFT targets, burning tokens from VE
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: VE balance drops below sum of other users' normal locked amounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Other users' withdraw() calls revert due to insufficient balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Attacker needs significant capital but permanently loses their tokens

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: VotingEscrow becomes insolvent for normal lock withdrawals
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users unable to withdraw expired locks — funds trapped
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires external token injection or contract upgrade

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Large capital for griefing (tokens are permanently burned)
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic — merge function is publicly callable

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: VE token balance < sum of non-smNFT locked amounts

threats.title: Delegation Replay via Stale Nonce — delegate() Does Not Invalidate Prior Signatures
threats.hypothesis_description: The delegate() function changes the delegatee but does NOT increment nonces[msg.sender]. Only delegateBySig increments nonces. An attacker who received a previously signed delegation message can submit it at any future time to override the signatory's subsequent delegate() calls. Alice signs delegation to Charlie (nonce 0), later calls delegate(Bob) (nonce stays 0), Charlie submits old signature (nonce 0 still valid) → Alice's delegation forced back to Charlie.
threats.investigation_rationale: The delegateBySig feature exists for gasless delegation via relayers. Users share signed messages with relayers, creating a window where signatures can be replayed after the user manually changes delegation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delegate() calls _delegate() without incrementing nonces[msg.sender]; delegateBySig checks `nonce == nonces[signatory]++` at line 800

preconditions: Victim has previously signed a delegateBySig message
Attacker possesses the signed message (shared via relayer or direct communication)
Victim subsequently changed delegation via delegate() (nonce not incremented)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: delegate(Bob) does not touch nonces[Alice]; old signature with nonce=0 for Charlie is still valid
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin mechanism to invalidate signatures
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent until victim uses delegateBySig (which would increment nonce)

operational_questions: Is delegateBySig used in production (relayer/gasless delegation)?
How many users have used delegate() after previously using delegateBySig?
validation_requirements: Confirm delegate() does not increment nonces
Test replay: sign delegation, call delegate(), submit old signature

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker receives signed delegation from victim (via relayer, dApp, etc.)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker waits for victim to call delegate() to change delegatee
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker submits old signed delegation — nonce still valid
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's delegation is silently overridden, redirecting all voting power
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires possession of a previously-signed delegation message

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Governance voting power can be hijacked for any signer who shared delegations
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Governance proposals can pass/fail based on stolen delegation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Victim must call delegateBySig to self (incrementing nonce) to permanently fix

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None — uses victim's existing voting power
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after victim changes delegation via delegate()

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: delegateBySig transactions overriding recent delegate() calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected delegation changes for users who called delegate()

threats.title: RewardsDistributor claim_many Missing AVM Owner Resolution Causes Rebase Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: RewardsDistributor.claim_many() does not resolve AVM original owners for expired locks. For expired non-permanent locks, it sends rewards directly to ownerOf(tokenId) which is the AutoVotingEscrow contract. The single claim() function correctly checks `avm.tokenIdToAVMId(_tokenId) != 0` and resolves via getOriginalOwner(), but claim_many completely omits this logic. Rebase rewards for AVM-managed expired locks are sent to AVE contracts with no recovery.
threats.investigation_rationale: claim() and claim_many() should produce identical behavior, but claim_many lacks the AVM routing logic present in claim(). Anyone can call claim_many for any token, routing AVM rewards incorrectly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claim_many: `address _nftOwner = IVotingEscrow(_voting_escrow).ownerOf(_tokenId)` without AVM check; contrast with claim() lines 130-140 which checks avm.tokenIdToAVMId

preconditions: AVM-managed veNFT has an expired lock (non-permanent, non-smNFT)
Rebase rewards have accumulated for the tokenId
claim_many is called (by anyone) with the AVM-managed tokenId

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: claim_many sends to ownerOf (AVE contract); claim sends to avm.getOriginalOwner (user). Different routing for same token.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin function to redirect or recover misrouted rebases
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time an AVM-managed lock expires and claim_many is called

operational_questions: Are there AVM-managed tokens with expired non-permanent locks?
Has claim_many been called for AVM-managed tokens?
Is there an automated process that calls claim_many?
validation_requirements: Compare claim() vs claim_many() output for same AVM-managed expired tokenId
Verify AVE contract has no token recovery function

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User enables auto-voting with a non-permanent lock
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Anyone calls claim_many([tokenId]) — rewards sent to AVE contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — anyone can call claim_many for any tokenId

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Rebase rewards permanently lost for affected AVM users
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: AVM users with expiring locks lose rebase income
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Rebase distribution for AVM-managed expired locks

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: BLACK tokens accumulating in AVE contracts from claim_many calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatched rebase distributions between claim and claim_many paths

threats.title: Last-Hour Voting Restriction Bypass via deposit_for Triggering Poke for Any Voted Token
threats.hypothesis_description: VoterV3.vote() restricts voting in the last hour of an epoch (epochVoteEnd check) for non-whitelisted, non-AVM tokens. However, VoterV3.poke() lacks this restriction — it only checks epochVoteStart. VotingEscrow.deposit_for() has no authentication requirement and triggers poke() for any voted token. Since msg.sender for poke() is the VE contract (passes `msg.sender == _ve` check), anyone can force a vote weight recalculation during the restricted last hour by depositing 1 wei into any voted token.
threats.investigation_rationale: The epochVoteEnd restriction was designed to prevent last-minute vote sniping. The deposit_for→poke path completely bypasses this restriction, allowing vote weight manipulation after the voting window closes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: poke() only checks `_timestamp <= epochVoteStart` (line 139), NOT epochVoteEnd; contrast vote() which checks `_timestamp >= epochVoteEnd` (line 161)

preconditions: Target token has active votes in the current epoch
Current time is within the last-hour restriction window (epochVoteEnd to epochNext)
Attacker has at least 1 wei of BLACK token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: deposit_for(tokenId, 1) → voted[tokenId] is true → IVoter(voter).poke(tokenId) → poke calls _vote which recalculates weights
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin control over poke timing restrictions
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Last NO_VOTING_WINDOW (300 seconds) of each epoch

operational_questions: Can deposit_for with minimal value trigger meaningful weight changes?
What is the practical impact of recalculated weights in the last hour?
Does the timing align with AVM voting window?
validation_requirements: Confirm deposit_for(tokenId, 1) triggers poke during restricted window
Verify poke passes msg.sender == _ve check
Test weight recalculation magnitude with varying deposit sizes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies high-value voted tokens during the epoch
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker accumulates BLACK tokens during the voting window
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: In the last hour, attacker calls deposit_for(targetTokenId, largeAmount) — tokens go into target's lock permanently
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Poke recalculates vote weights with inflated balance, shifting gauge emissions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Capital required for meaningful weight change; tokens are locked permanently

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Last-minute gauge weight manipulation affects emission distribution
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain fix without upgrading VoterV3 or VotingEscrow

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Minimal (1 wei) for trigger; substantial for meaningful impact
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must be within epochVoteEnd to epochNext window (300 seconds with WEEK=1800)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: deposit_for calls during last-hour window followed by weight changes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Vote weight recalculations during restricted voting period
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Small deposits into others' tokens during last hour

threats.title: RewardsDistributor Permanent Token Loss from checkpoint_token 20-Iteration Cap with WEEK=1800
threats.hypothesis_description: RewardsDistributor._checkpoint_token() distributes incoming tokens across weekly periods using a loop capped at 20 iterations. With WEEK=1800 seconds, 20 iterations covers only 36,000 seconds (10 hours). If checkpoint_token is not called for >10 hours, the loop exits without distributing tokens to the missed periods. The undistributed tokens remain in the contract but are never assigned to any tokens_per_week slot, making them permanently unclaimable by users.
threats.investigation_rationale: checkpoint_token can only be called by the depositor role. A 10-hour gap due to bot downtime, network congestion, or operational failure permanently loses rebase tokens from distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = 0; i < 20; i++) { next_week = this_week + WEEK; ... } — if 20 iterations exhausted before reaching block.timestamp, remaining to_distribute is unassigned

preconditions: WEEK = 1800 seconds (confirmed)
checkpoint_token not called for >10 hours (20 * 1800 seconds)
Tokens have been deposited to RewardsDistributor during the gap

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: 20 iterations * 1800s = 36,000s (10h) max coverage; tokens arriving during gaps beyond 10h never enter tokens_per_week
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Only depositor can call checkpoint_token; no catch-up mechanism
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any >10 hour gap in checkpoint_token calls

operational_questions: What is the current checkpoint_token call frequency?
Has there been any >10 hour gap since deployment?
Is there automated infrastructure calling checkpoint_token?
validation_requirements: Simulate 12-hour gap and verify token distribution
Check if missed tokens are recoverable via subsequent calls
Verify token_last_balance tracking after gap

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Depositor bot experiences downtime for >10 hours
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Tokens arrive at RewardsDistributor during downtime
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: When checkpoint_token resumes, loop caps at 20 iterations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens for missed periods are never distributed — permanently stuck
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — operational failure triggers this automatically

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Permanent loss of rebase tokens from distribution
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users receive less rebase than entitled
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Lost tokens cannot be reassigned; only withdrawERC20 by owner can extract them

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Gap between last_token_time and block.timestamp exceeding 10 hours
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Reduced rebase claims after service interruptions

threats.title: Checkpoint Loop Staleness with WEEK=1800 Corrupts Global Voting Power After 5.3 Days
threats.hypothesis_description: VotingEscrow._checkpoint() uses a 255-iteration loop to update global point_history. With WEEK=1800, this covers only 255*1800 = 459,000 seconds (~5.3 days). If no checkpoint-triggering action (deposit, withdraw, merge, etc.) occurs for >5.3 days, the loop cannot fully catch up. The global slope/bias tracking becomes permanently desynchronized, causing totalSupply() to return incorrect values. This affects all reward distributions and governance quorum calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: With WEEK=1800, the 255-iteration limit designed for weekly epochs becomes dangerously restrictive. Low-activity periods exceeding 5.3 days permanently corrupt global accounting.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = 0; i < 255; ++i) { t_i += WEEK; ... } — 255 * 1800 = 459,000 seconds max catch-up; MAXTIME = 126,144,000 seconds

preconditions: No checkpoint-triggering action for >5.3 days
Tokens have slope_changes scheduled during the gap period
WEEK = 1800 seconds

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: If protocol has quiet period >5.3 days, next checkpoint only processes 255 weeks of slope changes, missing intermediate changes
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Public checkpoint() function exists but requires someone to call it frequently
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any period of >5.3 days without on-chain activity

operational_questions: What is the longest gap between checkpoint-triggering transactions?
Is there automated infrastructure calling checkpoint()?
How many slope_changes are scheduled in the future?
validation_requirements: Simulate 6-day gap and verify totalSupply accuracy
Check if multiple checkpoint() calls can recover
Verify governance quorum calculations after gap

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for or identify low-activity period (>5.3 days without VE interactions)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: After gap, totalSupply returns stale value (slope changes not processed)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use inflated/deflated totalSupply for governance quorum manipulation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Pass governance proposals with less voting power than actually required

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect totalSupply affects all reward proportions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Multiple checkpoint() calls may partially recover, but some slope data may be permanently lost
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Governance voting, reward distribution, all totalSupply-dependent calculations

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None for trigger; governance tokens needed to exploit stale quorum
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate — understanding of voting escrow mechanics
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires >5.3 days without any VE interaction

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large epoch gaps between point_history entries
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: totalSupply mismatch with sum of individual balances
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Governance proposals passing with unexpectedly low votes

threats.title: SMNFT_BONUS Change Creates Total Supply Inflation and Reward Dilution
threats.hypothesis_description: When team calls setSmNFTBonus, the global point_history immediately reflects the new bonus rate for ALL smNFTBalance via `calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance)` in _checkpoint. However, individual smNFT checkpoints retain their OLD stored smNFTBonus values until each token is individually re-checkpointed. This creates a divergence: totalSupply() is inflated (or deflated) relative to sum of individual balanceOfNFT() values. This dilutes all users' share of RewardsDistributor rebases (which divides by totalSupply).
threats.investigation_rationale: The mutable SMNFT_BONUS parameter affects both global and per-token calculations but updates them at different times, breaking the fundamental invariant that totalSupply equals the sum of individual voting powers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: last_point.smNFTBonus = calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) uses current SMNFT_BONUS; individual checkpoints at line 419 use stored values

preconditions: smNFTs exist in the system (smNFTBalance > 0)
team calls setSmNFTBonus with a different value
Existing smNFTs have not been re-checkpointed after the change

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setSmNFTBonus(2000) changes bonus from 10% to 20%. Global totalSupply immediately reflects 20% bonus on ALL smNFTBalance. Individual tokens still show 10% until re-checkpointed.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: team role can trigger via setSmNFTBonus
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Immediately after bonus change, persists until all smNFTs re-checkpoint

operational_questions: Has SMNFT_BONUS been changed since deployment?
What is the current smNFTBalance?
What is the magnitude of totalSupply vs sum(balanceOfNFT) discrepancy?
validation_requirements: Calculate totalSupply before and after setSmNFTBonus
Compare with sum of individual balanceOfNFT values
Quantify reward dilution impact

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: totalSupply immediately inflates, diluting all users' rebase share
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Individual smNFT holders don't benefit until re-checkpointed
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Non-smNFT holders' rebases are reduced proportionally to inflation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All users' rebase proportions are distorted

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setSmNFTBonus transactions; divergence between totalSupply and individual sum
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reduced rebase claims after bonus change

threats.title: PermissionsRegistry removeRole Corrupts Address-to-Role Mappings
threats.hypothesis_description: PermissionsRegistry.removeRole() has a bug in cleaning up _addressToRoles. It uses `_roles[_roles.length - 1]` (last element of the global roles array) instead of `_addressToRoles[rta[i]][_addressToRoles[rta[i]].length - 1]` for the swap-and-pop operation. This replaces the removed role entry in users' role lists with an unrelated role from the global array. While hasRole mapping is correctly cleared, the _addressToRoles view becomes inconsistent, and subsequent removeRoleFrom operations on affected addresses may behave incorrectly.
threats.investigation_rationale: The wrong array source in swap-and-pop corrupts tracking data. The loop also doesn't break after pop, risking out-of-bounds access or double-pop if array alignment shifts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _addressToRoles[rta[i]][k] = _roles[_roles.length -1] — should be _addressToRoles[rta[i]][_addressToRoles[rta[i]].length - 1]; loop does not break after pop

preconditions: blackMultisig calls removeRole for a role that has assigned addresses
Affected addresses have other roles in their _addressToRoles array
Global _roles array has other roles (always true in production)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: After removeRole('GAUGE_ADMIN'), addresses with GAUGE_ADMIN see unrelated roles in their addressToRole list
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Only blackMultisig can trigger via removeRole
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every removeRole call for roles with active members

operational_questions: Has removeRole ever been called in production?
Are subsequent removeRoleFrom calls affected by corrupted data?
Does off-chain tooling rely on addressToRole view function?
validation_requirements: Call removeRole for a role with assigned addresses
Verify _addressToRoles corruption
Test subsequent removeRoleFrom for affected addresses

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin removes a role via removeRole
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: _addressToRoles for affected addresses becomes corrupted with wrong roles
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Subsequent removeRoleFrom may fail or remove wrong roles
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Role management integrity compromised for affected addresses

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Role tracking data corrupted; off-chain systems misinformed
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Subsequent role management operations may fail
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires manual role reassignment for affected addresses
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All role management for affected addresses

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None — triggered by normal admin operation

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Role management failures after removeRole calls

threats.title: Bribe notifyRewardAmount Vulnerable to Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Desync
threats.hypothesis_description: Bribe.notifyRewardAmount() records the full `reward` amount in tokenRewardsPerEpoch without verifying the actual received token amount. For fee-on-transfer tokens (which are accepted if whitelisted as connectors via TokenHandler), the contract receives fewer tokens than recorded. This creates an accounting surplus that causes later getReward calls to fail with insufficient balance for the last claimers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol allows arbitrary connector tokens as bribe rewards. If any whitelisted connector token has transfer fees, bribe insolvency results. No received-amount delta check exists.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted as a connector in TokenHandler
threats.config_evidence: TokenHandler.sol: _whitelistConnector has no fee-on-transfer check; Bribes.sol line 225: tokenRewardsPerEpoch += reward without balance delta verification

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, this, reward) then tokenRewardsPerEpoch += reward — no balance delta check

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted as connector (isConnector = true)
Someone calls notifyRewardAmount with that token
Multiple users attempt to claim rewards

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: notifyRewardAmount(feeToken, 1000) → contract receives 990 (1% fee) → records 1000 → last claimer gets shortchanged by 10
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Governance must whitelist fee-on-transfer token as connector
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every notifyRewardAmount call for affected tokens

operational_questions: Are any whitelisted connector tokens fee-on-transfer?
What is the cumulative accounting desync?
Have any getReward calls failed due to insufficient balance?
validation_requirements: Audit all whitelisted connectors for fee-on-transfer behavior
Compare tokenRewardsPerEpoch sums with actual contract balances

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Last claimers' getReward reverts due to insufficient balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires specific token type to be whitelisted

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Bribe reward insolvency for affected tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin to inject tokens or update accounting
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Bribe reward claims for fee-on-transfer tokens

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Bribe contract balance < sum of tokenRewardsPerEpoch for specific tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Specific token types causing claim failures

threats.title: BlackClaims recoverERC20 Can Drain Active Season Reward Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: BlackClaims.recoverERC20() allows the owner to withdraw ANY ERC20 token including the reward token (BLACK) during an active claiming season. There is no restriction preventing withdrawal of the reward token while remaining_reward_amount > 0. This would cause subsequent claimAndStakeReward calls to revert due to insufficient balance, permanently denying users their seasonal rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: The recoverERC20 function transfers the entire balance of any token to the owner without checking if it's the active reward token. This creates a single-function path to drain all season rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: recoverERC20(tokenAddress_) transfers full balance without checking if tokenAddress_ == address(token) or if season.remaining_reward_amount > 0

preconditions: Owner or secondOwner calls recoverERC20 with the reward token address
Active season with remaining_reward_amount > 0
Users have unclaimed rewards

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: recoverERC20(address(token)) → transfers all BLACK balance → claimAndStakeReward → token.transfer fails → users cannot claim
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time during active claiming season

operational_questions: Who are the current owner and secondOwner addresses?
Is there an active season with unclaimed rewards?
Are there operational procedures preventing accidental recoverERC20 of reward token?
validation_requirements: Verify recoverERC20 has no reward token exclusion
Test claimAndStakeReward after recoverERC20 drains balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised or malicious owner calls recoverERC20(address(token))
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All reward tokens transferred to owner address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All subsequent claims revert — users permanently lose rewards
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires owner key compromise or malicious admin

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All unclaimed seasonal rewards permanently lost to users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner could return tokens manually, but no on-chain enforcement

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: recoverERC20 calls with reward token address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Claim failures after admin token movements
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Owner withdrawing reward tokens during active season
